stem:
Researchers randomly selected 800 adults from a large city and recorded how many hours per week each person exercises and each person's annual medical expenses. They found that people who exercise more than 5 hours per week had, on average, $1,200 less in annual medical expenses than those who exercise 5 hours or fewer per week. Which of the following conclusions is best supported by this study?

(Note: The 800 participants were randomly selected from city residents aged 18 and older.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing exercise to more than 5 hours per week causes adults in the city to reduce annual medical expenses by approximately $1,200.

choices.label: B
choices.text: In this sample of 800 adults, those who exercised more than 5 hours per week had, on average, $1,200 less in annual medical expenses than those who exercised 5 hours or fewer.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Among all adults in the city, those who exercise more than 5 hours per week have, on average, $1,200 less in annual medical expenses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exercise is the primary factor determining annual medical expenses for adults in the city.

explanation:
This is an observational study because the researchers recorded existing behaviors rather than assigning participants to exercise groups. In observational studies, researchers cannot establish causation — only association. The key distinction is between what the data show about the sample versus what can be inferred about the population or about cause-and-effect.

Choice B is the only fully defensible conclusion because it accurately restates what was directly measured: a difference in average medical expenses between two groups within the sample of 800 adults. This is a factual description of the sample data, requiring no inference beyond the study itself.

Choice A (causation claim) is invalid: because participants were not randomly assigned to exercise levels, unobserved confounding variables — such as pre-existing health conditions, income level, or diet — could explain both higher exercise rates and lower medical costs. Without random assignment, causation cannot be established.

Choice C (population inference) might seem valid given random selection, but it overstates the precision of the claim. The $1,200 figure describes the sample; inferring the exact figure holds for the full population without accounting for sampling variability goes beyond what is justified by the study design alone.

Choice D is unsupported: the study examined only one variable (exercise) and cannot determine that it is the "primary" factor among all possible contributors to medical expenses.

distractorRationale.A: Students who confuse association with causation will select this. Because exercise groups were not randomly assigned, confounding variables (e.g., income, pre-existing health status) may explain the observed difference, making a causal claim unjustified.
distractorRationale.B: Correct. This is a direct, accurate description of what was observed in the sample, requiring no causal reasoning or population inference.
distractorRationale.C: Students who correctly identify the random sampling but then over-apply it will select this. Random sampling justifies generalizing the direction of the association to the population, but the observational design and potential confounds prevent stating the exact dollar figure as a population fact.
distractorRationale.D: Students who overreach from a single association to a comprehensive claim about all factors will choose this. The study examined only the relationship between exercise and medical costs, providing no basis for comparing exercise to all other possible predictors.
